result:
Tonight I’m thinking about watch-repair shops. In the town where I grew up there was one on a sidestreet no wider than an afterthought, wedged between a flower stall that only sold white peonies and a tobacconist whose window display hadn’t changed since 1984. The shop had no name you could read in daylight; the gilt letters above the doorframe had surrendered long ago to rust and rain. Inside, whenever you pushed open the warped oak door, a bell the size of a cherry pit gave one exhausted ding. The man behind the counter—Mr. Rostova, though he never told anyone his first name—had eyeglasses perched so far down his nose they seemed to levitate. He smelled faintly of mineral oil and cardamom.

What kept people returning wasn’t that Mr. Rostova fixed timepieces—anyone can replace a battery or solder a hair-thin wire—but that he listened to their stories while he worked. Few customers came for the clocks themselves; most came because the clocks carried their dead. Grandmother’s locket watch, still lilting between 11:46 and 11:47 every afternoon. A father’s diver’s watch that stopped at the precise moment he surfaced from the quarry lake in August ’92. A child’s first Swatch, scuffed at the strap, its hands stuck on the minute the girl learned her parents were splitting up. Mr. Rostova never hurried. He let the owner hold the broken object and tell the tale once more, as if retelling could rewind the mechanism. In that dim shop the stories accumulated like oil on work rags, soaked and refracted through the magnifying loupe clamped to his forehead.

I remember visiting when I was twelve. My parents had sent me with a pocket watch belonging to my uncle, who had died the previous winter. The watch had frozen at 3:16. I didn’t know at the time that 3:16 is sometimes called “the mercy minute,” cited in hospitals as the moment most cardiac arrests are reversed—though in hospitals clocks are always set by committee, not tragedy. Mr. Rostova took the silver disk from my palm the way you receive a baby bird too stunned to fly.

While he worked, the walls ticked in heterodox rhythm—grandfather clocks, cuckoos, carriage pieces, ship’s chronometers, even a braille watch whose raised dots jittered when the minute hand leapt forward. He turned the loupe toward the gears and apologized to the tiny brass universe before coaxing it back into motion. The oil he used smelled greener than anything in January, like pine pitch left to cure inside a heart. When the second hand resumed its patient march, Mr. Rostova asked if I wanted to hear the watch’s secondhand heartbeat. I pressed its open back to my ear and discovered that time sounded nothing like the pulse of electronics in cartoons; it sounded like rain tapping leaf by leaf, almost apologetic.

He wound it until the crown clicked, then spun the watch on its chain like he was dredging a well of light. Twenty-seven years later I can still recall the angle of the fluorescent bulb glancing off that dial—how the numerals, once restored, looked freshly italicized, as if to say “we are traveling now, you and I.” Mr. Rostova wrapped the watch in crimson velvet and handed it back, telling me that keeping an object wound was a prayer in miniature. He charged my parents eight dollars, less than a round-trip bus ticket across town.

I went back only once, years later, after college and distant heartbreaks—covering the distance with the same steps but inside a stranger’s legs. The flower stall was shuttered, its white peonies replaced by graffiti of the word “Beloved” repeated in eight languages. The tobacconist had become a vape lounge. And the watch shop—well, the door was ajar, but the shelves were empty. No ticks, no green-oil scent. Just a single ornate clock on the counter, face removed, its heart cavity picked clean. I asked the new landlord what had happened. She said Mr. Rostova closed the shop early one Tuesday, left all the instruments arranged like surgical steel on blue cloth, and walked down the street humming “As Time Goes By.” She found the velvet pouch that once held my uncle’s watch looped around the minute hand of the exposed clock. Inside was a scrap of paper and a tiny brass gear the size of a lentil. The paper read: “Some watches are not for us to repair. Some belong only to those who will set them running again after us.”

I still have both items. The gear sits in the locket watch my daughter wore at her first piano recital—second hand on the downbeat, left hand on middle C. The scrap of paper is taped above the desk where I take apart half-written poems, line by line. It reminds me that every story has a mainspring, thin as regret and twice as stubborn; if you press ear to its rib cage you can almost hear it whir.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
